82 / 100 GIS Programming 2025

Weighted Spatial Redistribution

Resolving Spatial Ambiguity with Population-Weighted GIS

PythonGeoPandasRasterioNumPyskimage

Project Overview

This project implements and extends Jonny Huck's (2015) spatial weighted redistribution algorithm to resolve the false hotspot problem in spatially ambiguous data. Passively geocoded tweets are collapsed to a single centroid of the local administrative boundary, creating misleading hotspots. This project reimplements the algorithm to diffuse those points across each admin area, informed by a population density weighting surface.

The specific application: redistributing geocoded Twitter data about the Royal Wedding across Greater Manchester to visualise likely tweet origins and evaluate implications for advertising targeting strategies. This project achieved a grade of 82.

Motivation

Passive geocoding collapses all tweets from an area to a single centroid — an extreme form of spatial generalisation that creates artefactual hotspots where real population may not peak. This distortion is particularly problematic in marketing, urban planning, and emergency services, where spatial precision drives resource allocation decisions.

I explored how to translate methodological ambiguity into a computational workflow, achieving efficient raster-based processing while maintaining visual and statistical validity.

Key Design Decisions

Spatial Indexing

Leveraged GeoPandas' sindex with R-trees to filter candidate points in logarithmic time, using within() checks to confirm geometry containment.

Efficient Geometry Filtering

Random seeds were batch-generated within bounding boxes and validated in vectorised NumPy operations, avoiding redundant loops and minimising memory overhead.

Raster Updates via Disk Buffers

Used skimage.draw.disk() to localise Euclidean decay calculations, avoiding computationally expensive full-raster iteration on every seed placement.

Sensitivity Analysis

The 12-panel figure was created dynamically to visualise how the algorithm responds to changes in the s parameter (spatial ambiguity) and W (number of seed samples):

  • Lower s values produce tight clusters — suitable for fine-resolution targeting (e.g., billboards)
  • Higher s values broaden distributions — ideal for coarse targeting like regional radio
  • Larger W values favour high-density areas; low W introduces greater stochastic spread

Challenges Overcome

  • MAUP and Scale Discrepancy: The tweet data's Level 3 admin areas didn't perfectly align with the redistribution shapefile, revealing critical spatial assumptions buried in generalisation pipelines.
  • Visualising Uncertainty: Rather than claim precision, the algorithm visualises likelihood — outputs are better suited to qualitative inference than point prediction.
  • Runtime Efficiency: Code executes in under 10 seconds, enabling multiple model runs for the 12-panel comparative output without memory bloat.

Reflections

This project demonstrates a solid understanding of spatial data uncertainty, Python-based geospatial workflows, and efficient algorithm design. Investing time in optimising for usability, flexibility, and clarity produced skills that translate directly into real-world GIS roles where data quality and uncertainty communication are critical.

Resources

Skills Demonstrated

  • Spatial data uncertainty modelling
  • Raster processing & disk buffers
  • Vectorised NumPy operations
  • Algorithm efficiency & profiling
  • Dynamic multi-panel visualisation
  • MAUP and scale analysis
